Voice or how to build a $90 million business with one letter? Hi, it's Kevin Halbert. In the first halbertology video, we covered the first two parts of the base trilogy, market and message. Now it's time to share with you the most elusive of the three pillars of the base trilogy. Voice. You probably think voice refers to tone, and it does to a degree. But it's not just about tone. It's about the entire presentation. The tone you write to your prospects in, the language you use, the look and feel of your message, the overall personality of your presentation. We have to look and sound completely in tune with our prospects' lifestyles and personalities in order to connect with them. The old method of just getting noticed and heard no longer is good enough. Now you must give them a look they immediately identify with and speak to them in a voice that communicates in their own lingo. You can thank social media for that. To do that, you must write like they speak, use words they use, and even have a look about your marketing materials that they are familiar and comfortable with. The key to successful ad writing is preparation and research. I've already helped you to ask better questions to research your market and customers' needs. Now I'm going to give you the basics of how to start telling your story in a compelling and genuine fashion through copy that could ensure fantastic response rates many times over that of your competition. There's a reason this course is in black words on a white background. Words are the currency of strong advertising. Yes, a picture is worth a thousand of them, and good art and video can greatly enhance any offer you make. But the copy, the story, is what will ultimately separate you from the crowd. In our full course of howvertology, we will go in depth on the marriage of art and copy and how to use social media and strong advertising copy to tell your story in multiple platforms to the broadest audience. Also, how to help narrow your sales funnel to only target the highest quality leads. Allow me to digress for a moment into traffic and conversion. One of the biggest fallacies in marketing today is if you pepper social media with your message everywhere all the time, you'll bring in business. That is not true. You might drive a bit of traffic initially to your page, but what will a bunch of looky lose with no defined need for your product do? They will scroll and click right on by. And soon, your bird shot marketing strategy will be nothing more than more noisy static that irritates your customers more than it entices them. If every single person on Twitter read your hashtag, would your web copy be ready to sell them your product when they land? You do not want to sell every person on Twitter, but instead you want to drive the right people to your website with their hands already pulling out their credit cards because they are sure they need what you are selling, which brings me back to voice. Your copy must convince the buyers that they must have your product and convince the looky lose to get out fast. You do not want every single lead. You want every high quality lead who will buy. And you want them clicking the buy now button as fast and with as much certainty as you can. Before we get into the what that goes into your ad or sales letter, let's talk about the voice or how you speak with them. As a rule, always keep your language simple. In the past, tests have concluded that a seventh grade reading level is what we are after. Now, new tests are saying that even dropping into the fifth grade level may boost conversions. Are people that illiterate? No, they aren't. They just like things easy, that's all. And reading at a fifth or seventh grade level is easy. This is far from an absolute rule, however. If you are targeting a group of rocket scientists, you have to speak their language. Just like if you are talking with stay at home moms, you have to speak in theirs. The same is true with CEOs, factory workers, software programmers, restaurant owners, lumber jacks, dog catchers, you name it. You must make sure the voice or tone of your message matches your audience. The voice of your advertising is crucial. Madison Avenue has spent decades giving customers a shorthand of expectations based on voice, tone, and authority. You can use that shorthand to your advantage in a lot of ways. You may think that your voice is just you talking to your customer, but it's not. It's how you want your brand to talk to them. How does your brand speak? Is it conversational, hip, educational, authoritative? That depends on both your product and your customer. For instance, McDonald's has been the home of the fast and cheap burger for decades. But as they see their market share fall, they are trying any and everything to keep their brand front and center. In urban centers, they've relied heavily on tone. But in national ad campaigns like their recent Signs ads during the Golden Globes, they went for straight on sentimentality and the fact that there's a McD's in nearly every part of the world. However, in both of these cases, the franchise giant has come under fire for missing the mark, the right tone for their intended audiences. In one of the recent urban ads featuring the double cheeseburger, the copy read, I'd hit that, prompting more than one ad washer to suggest McDonald's consult urban dictionary before attempting more slang. The Signs campaign came under fire for using natural disasters and even 9-11 to drive brand sentiment. Their traffic, particularly on Twitter, increased, but the additional 15,000 hits were largely negative, setting customer distaste for making a buck off of tragedy. The point is that even as McDonald's miss fires, they are attempting to niche market with tone and voice. They will eventually get it right and speak to their varied markets in the language each understands. If your brand is going for the NPR listener, you might want an authoritative tone with lots of facts and interesting bits of trivia because that customer loves to know the inside story. However, if your customer base is bass fisherman, they want to talk bait lines and the best places to catch fish without a lot of fanfare. Your presentation also has to have the right look and personality. For example, the bass fisherman wants to see pictures of big bass and the lures that caught them. They want to feel like they are hearing a blistering hot fishtail from one of their best fishing buddies. The NPR listener likes to feel they are getting stories, personalities and interesting intellectual nuggets they can use and pass on. They want to feel they've learned something. You get the picture, make your ad, website, sales letter or whatever marketing materials you put out there resonate with your best prospect in what it says, how it says it and how it looks. Now let's talk about writing your ad copy. My dad had plenty to say about it and if you have ever read his letters, you know he told you point blank how to write great copy. Why did he do that? Why would he give away the secrets of his success? Because he knew that only those who truly wanted to learn it, who didn't just want but needed to achieve copywriting success would put his words into action. Sorry buckwheat, it doesn't work that way. If you really want to know it, you really got to do it. Gary Halbert. I'm going to share some of those secrets with you today because you are obviously one of the people who is passionate about growing your business, expanding your markets and transforming your life. Yeah, it might sound hunky, but writing a great marketing piece can and should do all of those things and more. Like bringing customers who, as Frank Kern said, will gladly pay you 300% more for your products just because they're yours. I promised you a $90 million letter and here it is, my dad's world famous Code of Arms letter. 5687 Ira Road. Bath, Ohio. 44210. Phone. 1216-666-9356. Dear Mr. McDonald, did you know that your family name was recorded with a code of arms in ancient heraldic archives more than seven centuries ago? My husband and I discovered this while doing some research for some friends of ours who have the same last name as you do. We've had an artist recreate the code of arms exactly as described in the ancient records. This drawing, along with other information about the name, has been printed up into an attractive one page report. The bottom half of the report tells the story of the very old and distinguished family name of McDonald. It tells what the name means, its origin, the original family motto, its place in history, and about famous people who share it. The top half has a large, beautiful re-production of an artist's drawing of the early snowing code of arms for the name of McDonald. This entire report is documented, authentic, and printed on parchment-like paper, suitable for framing. The report so delighted our friends that we've had a few extra copies made in order to share this information with other people of the same name. Framed, these reports make distinctive wall decorations, and they are great gifts for relatives. It should be remembered that we have not traced anyone's individual family tree, but have researched back through several centuries to find out about the earliest people named McDonald. All we are asking for them is enough to cover the added expenses of having the extra copies printed and mailed. See below. If you are interested, please let us know right away as our supply is pretty slim. Just verify that we have your correct name and address and send the correct amount in cash or check for the number of reports you want. We'll send them promptly by return mail. Sincerely, Nancy L. Halbert. P.S. If you are ordering only one report, send two dollars. Additional reports ordered at the same time and sent to the same address are one dollar each. That is the single direct mail piece that is a cornerstone of my dad's empire. What do you think? This letter has been copied thousands of times and studied for four decades by ace copywriters and markers the world over to discover the secret of its success. I'm going to tell you that secret right now. It's simple, plain spoken, absolutely anti-height, and addresses that core human need for status with an elegant offer. It's the perfect marriage of market, message, and voice. It may in fact be perfect. It is the anti-sales sales letter. Everything from the street address to the hand canceled stamp was designed to scream personal communication. And it passed the sniff test to most customers, meaning it seemed to come from someone's home, not a company. This direct address to the reader is sincere, kind-hearted, and open. It's coming at you from small town Ohio, which is non-threatening, and has a broad appeal because it's offering an informational report on your family's history and a peek at your personal coat of arms. It's not much more significant in the status and belonging categories than learning your family has an ancient, heraldic lineage, and then offering it to you in a report that you can frame, put on the wall, and show to the world. It also uses the customer's individual surname and was customized for each person he mailed it to. Dad was so good that he knew a woman would be a much better character to sell this heraldic arms report than he would, so he wrote it as if he was our mom. That is some masterful voicing right there. At one point, he was mailing one million of these letters a week and took in as much as 20,000 checks a day. Not even adjusting for inflation. At $2 per check and 20,000 checks per day, that's a smooth $40,000 each day. Dad was bringing in that kind of cash from this one letter in the early 1970s, and it still stands as a titan among direct response letters some 40 years later. As he said, it is packed with psychology that is not apparent to the naked eye or the naked mind. It should. He spent 18 months crafting it. Every bit of the expertise contained in that letter still applies to direct marketing today. If I had the time today, I would impact this masterpiece line by line, but we're limited in these short videos, so I'll try to make some of the big points. Even if you aren't using a hand-canceled, stamped envelope, your email subject lines can act as stand-ins. Your copy can make realistic, easily debunked claims which you are then prepared to back up with product and proof. Your message is targeted geographically and or demographically to your market. The writing is simple, clean, and concise, and at about a fifth grade reading level, and your offer is something that seems easy. Buying is not a hard decision at all for your customer to make. If you wanted a direct marketing piece that could promise huge sales, you could template off of Dad's Code of Arms letter. Enter your own sympathetic narrator, your own product and price point, and tailor make your message to each customer. You can do far worse than to adapt and adopt some of the greatest copy in the world by one of the best who has ever lived. But I really want you to do better, to be a better marketer for your brand than you have ever imagined. So here's even more. If you want to go more in depth for the process, you can follow in Dad's footsteps with the process I'm going to outline below. The breakdown for the first steps in writing a great ad was to really sit down and look at slash think about your product or service and write a complete fact sheet. On this sheet, you include every attribute, great and small of your product. No prospective customer will ever see your fact sheet. It exists to make sure you understand your product completely. From the fact sheet, you then create the benefits list. This list is not of the attributes of your product, but instead, ask you to consider what benefits the attributes give to your customer. Obviously, not every fact can translate into a benefit. But if you're really doing the work, you should be able to find several dozen benefits that you've translated from your facts. Next, you need to design your offer. This is the second most crucial part of the ad writing process. This is where the magic starts to happen. You've done the prep, and now you can start really writing the ad. Yes, everything I just took you through above is preparation, not ad writing yet. Here's the secret no one outside of the professional copy and marketing world wants you to know. We steal happily from each other, and we do it all the time. We even help each other steal by providing our best content, just like I'm doing with you in these short videos. Remember though, like dad said, you have to do the work to get the results. As to his coat of arms letter, a lot of grade A work was already done for you, but you still have to translate it to your product or service. Someone recently shared with me a study that showed that nearly any person could become an expert in any subject if they read seven excellent books on it. My dad thought that there were nine books that everyone interested in marketing and copy had to read. Some of them, like scientific advertising, are expensive, but worth their weight in gold. Luckily for you, dad read them all, and Bond and I grew up with them. You may still want to read all of dad's suggested reading list, but we've already done that work for you. I would certainly recommend reading all of what dad suggested and more, but for now it might be much faster for you to pick our brains. Did you know that there's an almost foolproof trick to writing a raving fan making copy that is completely unfair, totally legal, and practiced by almost every marketing and copy expert in the world? Allow me to introduce you to another time-saving marketing device, the swipe file. Every professional copywriter and marketer has a swipe file. That is where you keep full ads, headlines, and sales letters so that you can go back and mine them for gold. You do not lift the copy directly from a successful letter and paste it into yours. That is plagiarism and not the kind of stealing I meant at all. What I mean is that an idea and an ad can spur you to inspiration and productive killer copy, and you can even template your letter off the one you swiped, like the coat of arms letter. This is where research comes in again. You can start this moment to subscribe to any number of newsletters and online publications for your niche market. When a headline, slice of copy, or whole piece speaks to you and makes you want to give that company money, swipe that file. Here are some fantastic examples of controls. A control is copy that beats all the other copy written in getting your customers to opt in, click through, and most importantly, to buy. Find these ads. They are available all over the internet and copy them into your swipe file. Do you make these mistakes in English? What everybody should know about the stock and bond business? The Nancy L. Halbert Heraldry letter, the coat of arms letter contained here. How to burn off body fat hour by hour. At 60 miles an hour, the loudest noise in this Rolls Royce is the ticking of the electric clock. Why men crack. How to collect social security at any age. The Admiral Bird Transpolar Expedition letter. The lazy man's way to riches. Truly great controls, like the coat of arms letter, which sell product for years, for decades, are called Evergreen, and they are the goal of every great marketer. Once you have an Evergreen product and control copy, you have created what my friend Mark McGray calls a cash machine. A product that is so entrenched in its niche, it makes you money with very little tending. That's the reason why the coat of arms and the Schwartz Wall Street Journal letter, among others, are hugely famous and studied to this day. They are true Evergreen controls that fortunes are made from. Later, when you are crafting your message and copy, you can look at your swipe file anytime and riff off one of the ads you see. Oh, and if you don't already get the Gary Halbert letters, when you sign up with halbertology, you can. Here's the thing. Dad knew that the only way to write a great ad or create a killer campaign was to do the legwork and we're laying it all out for you in the short video. But my time with you is limited, so I can really only hit the highest points. You should be reading those newsletters you subscribe to, a bit of the books you've gotten, and looking through your swipe file every day. I assure you that after just a few days of reading the best advertising copy in the world, you will begin seeing copy and marketing in a whole new light. It will become almost effortless for you to identify great copy from copy that is merely good. And you will identify bad copy immediately. You will begin to realize that copy is actually everywhere in all aspects of marketing. And by using what you are learning here, you will be able to transcend mediocre messages and lukewarm offers to create the most effective offers that drive your market to need your product. So that by the time you have issued your call to action, they are drooling to hit that buy button. We'll put it all together for you in our next video. But for now, the most important thing is to just write and write some more and some more. See you next time.
